decision: How should the application's HTTP surface be shaped: a server-rendered multi-page application, a REST/JSON API backend consumed by a single-page frontend, or a hybrid approach?

grounding.existing_system: n/a — greenfield (C3): no existing system to conform to
grounding.note: arch-canon.json absent; option set reasoned from established architecture first principles; currency self-verified; A1 (server-side PDF file-streaming endpoint) is compatible with all three candidates; INV6 (single-server synchronous) does not eliminate any candidate

options.summary: The server renders full HTML pages on each request; browser navigates between pages with standard HTTP GET/POST; no separate frontend build artifact.
options.reason_to_consider: Simplest delivery model for a personal-tool web app: eliminates a separate frontend build pipeline, stays in one deployable unit (consistent with DP1 monolith options and INV6), and every major DP2 framework stack (Rails ERB, Django templates, Express+EJS) has strong server-rendering support.

strengths: Single deployable unit — no frontend build step or separate SPA hosting required; consistent with INV6 (single-server) and simplifies CI/CD under C2.
Server-rendered HTML is directly compatible with A1's server-side PDF file-streaming endpoint (the PDF endpoint is just another server route returning a file instead of HTML).
Full-page navigation is standard-browser-compatible, satisfying AC1 (no native app install) with zero client-side framework overhead.
Simpler security surface: session cookies and CSRF tokens are straightforward in MPA; no need for JWT/CORS configuration.
Faster time to first visible content for the feature set (CRUD forms, time-entry list, project list) at A13 scale.
costs: Full-page reloads on every interaction; form submissions and list updates require round trips, which is more noticeable on slower connections though acceptable at personal-tool scale (A13).
UI interactivity (inline editing, dynamic filtering) requires additional JavaScript or HTMX-style progressive enhancement if desired; pure MPA is less interactive than an SPA.
If a richer frontend is added later, migrating from server-rendered templates to an API-driven approach requires significant refactoring.

options.option: REST/JSON API backend with a single-page application frontend
options.summary: The server exposes a stateless REST/JSON API; a separate JavaScript SPA (React, Vue, Svelte, etc.) consumes it from the browser; two distinct build artifacts.
options.reason_to_consider: REST API + SPA is the dominant pattern for interactive web apps in 2025; provides a clean separation of concerns between server logic and UI, and enables richer client-side interactivity (inline time-entry editing, dynamic filtering) that CRUD-heavy tools often benefit from.

strengths: Clean contract between backend and frontend — the JSON API is independently testable and allows the frontend to evolve without touching server logic.
Enables rich client-side interactivity (inline edit, optimistic updates, instant list refresh) without full-page reloads.
SPA frameworks (React, Vue, Svelte) have large ecosystems and strong tooling support.
The JSON API surface makes future integrations (mobile client, third-party scripts) straightforward without additional server work.
costs: Two separate build pipelines (server and SPA) increase initial setup complexity — more work to get to a working skeleton under C2.
Introduces a second deployment artifact or a bundling step to serve the SPA from the same origin as the API — more configuration than a pure MPA.
REST API requires explicit CORS policy and token-based session (or cookie/CSRF adaptation) for cross-origin requests; adds auth surface complexity.
For simple CRUD at A13 scale, the SPA framework's bundle size and JS overhead may exceed the actual UI complexity of the application.

A1's server-side PDF file-streaming endpoint still requires a dedicated server route; the SPA simply triggers a link navigation to it — no architectural blocker, but it is a separate integration point.

options.option: Hybrid: server-rendered HTML with API endpoints for dynamic interactions (e.g., HTMX or light SPA islands)
options.summary: Primary UI is server-rendered HTML; targeted API or partial-HTML endpoints handle specific dynamic interactions (inline updates, partial page refreshes) using HTMX, Turbo Streams, or similar; single deployable unit.
options.reason_to_consider: Hybrid approaches (HTMX + server-rendered HTML, Rails Hotwire/Turbo, Django + HTMX) have become a mainstream production pattern as of 2024–2025 for exactly this type of CRUD-heavy personal tool; provides MPA simplicity with selective interactivity without a full SPA build pipeline.

strengths: Retains single-process deployment simplicity (no separate SPA artifact) — consistent with INV6 and DP1 monolith options.
Adds targeted interactivity (partial page updates, inline form submission) without a full frontend framework and without full-page reloads for every action.
HTMX/Turbo are lightweight JavaScript libraries (no build step required for basic usage) — lower frontend tooling overhead than a full SPA under C2.
A1's PDF streaming endpoint is a standard server route, compatible with no changes regardless of hybrid strategy.
Gradual adoption path: start MPA-pure for the skeleton, add HTMX interactions slice by slice without architectural rework.
costs: Introduces a dependency on a client-side interaction library (HTMX, Turbo) whose mental model differs from both pure MPA and pure SPA — team must learn and maintain it.

Partial-HTML endpoint responses (HTMX fragments, Turbo Streams) create a two-response-format convention (full HTML for navigation, partial HTML for updates) that must be consistently applied across slices.
Debugging hybrid interaction flows (partial render, swap target, event handling) is more complex than debugging either a pure MPA or a pure REST/SPA interaction.
Less widely understood than the REST+SPA pattern; fewer developers have production experience with HTMX or Turbo compared to React/Vue.

no_pick_note: Live, unranked alternatives. EVALUATE-DECIDE scores against CONSTRAINTS / ACCEPTANCE / NFRs and picks (role 4); OPTION-GEN does not recommend.
